Estos son los hallazgos clave de los nuevos tests:

---

**Lo bueno — el filtro Lun+Vie funciona:**

IS: PF 1.24 → OOS: PF 1.34. La estrategia *mejoró* de IS a OOS en lugar de degradar, que es exactamente lo que quieres ver. WR estable en 60-62% en ambos períodos, racha máxima de pérdidas idéntica (4 en ambos). El borde es real y consistente en la dimensión de días.

**El problema que persiste — el DD:**

IS DD: 5.24-5.81% (borde del límite). OOS DD: 6.34-6.97% (viola el límite de FundingPips). El problema ya no son los días — eso está resuelto. El problema ahora es **Lunes SELL**, que es el único segmento que degrada consistentemente de IS (+$77) a OOS (-$210). Sin él, el OOS hubiera tenido DD significativamente menor.

**El hallazgo más importante de estos datos:**

Viernes SELL es robusto en ambos períodos (IS=+$182, OOS=+$203). Lunes SELL no lo es. Esto elimina la hipótesis de "quitar todos los SELLs" — solo necesitas bloquear Lunes SELL, probablemente con un filtro de tendencia H4.

**Siguiente paso concreto:**

El código de ORB_v3 necesita una sola adición: si es Lunes y el precio está por encima de MA(50) en H4, cancelar el SELL stop antes de que se active. Eso proyecta eliminar ~$200-280 en pérdidas OOS y debería llevar el DD a zona del 4%. ¿Arrancamos con ese código?

content.name: bash_tool

input.command:
cd /home/claude && python3 << 'EOF'
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

def find_deals_row(filepath):
    df = pd.read_excel(filepath, header=None)
    for i in range(len(df)):
        val = str(df.iloc[i, 0]) if pd.notna(df.iloc[i, 0]) else ''
        if 'Transacciones' in val:
            return i
    return None

def extract_trades(filepath):
    dr = find_deals_row(filepath)
    df_raw = pd.read_excel(filepath, header=None)
    deals_df = df_raw.iloc[dr+1:].reset_index(drop=True)
    deals_df.columns = ['DateTime','Deal','Symbol','Type','Direction','Volume','Price','Order','Commission','Swap','Profit','Balance','Comment']
    all_xau = deals_df[deals_df['Symbol'] == 'XAUUSD'].copy()
    all_xau['DateTime'] = pd.to_datetime(all_xau['DateTime'])
    all_xau['Profit'] = pd.to_numeric(all_xau['Profit'], errors='coerce')
    all_xau['Price'] = pd.to_numeric(all_xau['Price'], errors='coerce')
    all_xau['Balance'] = pd.to_numeric(all_xau['Balance'], errors='coerce')
    
    ins = all_xau[all_xau['Direction']=='in'][['DateTime','Type','Price']].rename(
        columns={'DateTime':'EntryTime','Type':'TradeDir','Price':'EntryPrice'}).reset_index(drop=True)
    outs = all_xau[all_xau['Direction']=='out'].copy().reset_index(drop=True)
    outs['TradeDir'] = ins['TradeDir'].values[:len(outs)]
    outs['EntryPrice'] = ins['EntryPrice'].values[:len(outs)]
    outs['Comment'] = outs['Comment'].astype(str)
    outs['ExitReason'] = outs['Comment'].apply(lambda x: 'TP' if 'tp' in x.lower() else ('SL' if 'sl' in x.lower() else 'Other'))
    outs['DayOfWeek'] = outs['DateTime'].dt.day_name()
    outs['Month'] = outs['DateTime'].dt.month
    outs['Year'] = outs['DateTime'].dt.year
    outs['Quarter'] = outs['DateTime'].dt.quarter
    outs['Win'] = outs['Profit'] > 0
    return outs

bk = extract_trades('/mnt/user-data/uploads/ReportTester-Bk.xlsx')
fr = extract_trades('/mnt/user-data/uploads/ReportTester-fr.xlsx')

def stats(df):
    if len(df)==0: return {'n':0,'wr':0,'pf':0,'net':0,'avgW':0,'avgL':0}
    wins=df['Win'].sum(); total=len(df)
    gw=df[df['Profit']>0]['Profit'].sum()
    gl=abs(df[df['Profit']<0]['Profit'].sum())
    pf=gw/gl if gl>0 else 999
    avgW=df[df['Profit']>0]['Profit'].mean() if wins>0 else 0
    avgL=df[df['Profit']<0]['Profit'].mean() if (total-wins)>0 else 0
    return {'n':total,'wr':wins/total*100,'pf':pf,'net':df['Profit'].sum(),'avgW':avgW,'avgL':avgL}

print("=== BACKTEST (Bk) - Jan 2024 → Feb 2026 | Lun+Vie only ===")
s=stats(bk); print(f"TOTAL: n={s['n']}, WR={s['wr']:.1f}%, PF={s['pf']:.3f}, Net=${s['net']:.0f}")
print(f"Period: {bk['DateTime'].min().date()} → {bk['DateTime'].max().date()}")

print("\n=== FORWARD (fr) - Jan 2025 → Feb 2026 | Lun+Vie only ===")
s=stats(fr); print(f"TOTAL: n={s['n']}, WR={s['wr']:.1f}%, PF={s['pf']:.3f}, Net=${s['net']:.0f}")
print(f"Period: {fr['DateTime'].min().date()} → {fr['DateTime'].max().date()}")

# By day
print("\n=== BY DAY ===")
for day in ['Monday','Friday']:
    b=stats(bk[bk['DayOfWeek']==day]); f=stats(fr[fr['DayOfWeek']==day])
    print(f"{day}: BK n={b['n']} WR={b['wr']:.1f}% PF={b['pf']:.3f} Net=${b['net']:.0f} | FR n={f['n']} WR={f['wr']:.1f}% PF={f['pf']:.3f} Net=${f['net']:.0f}")

# By direction
print("\n=== BY DIRECTION ===")
for d in ['buy','sell']:
    b=stats(bk[bk['TradeDir']==d]); f=stats(fr[fr['TradeDir']==d])
    print(f"{d.upper()}: BK n={b['n']} WR={b['wr']:.1f}% PF={b['pf']:.3f} Net=${b['net']:.0f} | FR n={f['n']} WR={f['wr']:.1f}% PF={f['pf']:.3f} Net=${f['net']:.0f}")

# Day x Direction
print("\n=== DAY x DIRECTION ===")
for day in ['Monday','Friday']:
    for d in ['buy','sell']:
        b=stats(bk[(bk['DayOfWeek']==day)&(bk['TradeDir']==d)])
        f=stats(fr[(fr['DayOfWeek']==day)&(fr['TradeDir']==d)])
        print(f"  {day} {d.upper()}: BK n={b['n']} WR={b['wr']:.1f}% PF={b['pf']:.3f} Net=${b['net']:.0f} | FR n={f['n']} WR={f['wr']:.1f}% PF={f['pf']:.3f} Net=${f['net']:.0f}")

# By quarter
print("\n=== BY QUARTER ===")
for q in [1,2,3,4]:
    b=stats(bk[bk['Quarter']==q]); f=stats(fr[fr['Quarter']==q])
    print(f"Q{q}: BK n={b['n']} WR={b['wr']:.1f}% PF={b['pf']:.3f} Net=${b['net']:.0f} | FR n={f['n']} WR={f['wr']:.1f}% PF={f['pf']:.3f} Net=${f['net']:.0f}")

# By month
print("\n=== BY MONTH ===")
mnames=['Jan','Feb','Mar','Apr','May','Jun','Jul','Aug','Sep','Oct','Nov','Dec']
for m in range(1,13):
    b=stats(bk[bk['Month']==m]); f=stats(fr[fr['Month']==m])
    if b['n']==0 and f['n']==0: continue
    print(f"{mnames[m-1]}: BK n={b['n']} WR={b['wr']:.1f}% PF={b['pf']:.3f} Net=${b['net']:.0f} | FR n={f['n']} WR={f['wr']:.1f}% PF={f['pf']:.3f} Net=${f['net']:.0f}")

# Exit reasons
print("\n=== EXIT REASONS ===")
for name,df in [('BK',bk),('FR',fr)]:
    print(f"{name}: {df['ExitReason'].value_counts().to_dict()}")

# Max DD
print("\n=== DRAWDOWN ANALYSIS ===")
for name,df in [('BK',bk),('FR',fr)]:
    profits=df.sort_values('DateTime')['Profit'].values
    peak=0; maxdd=0; running=0; dd_list=[]
    for p in profits:
        running+=p
        if running>peak: peak=running
        dd=peak-running
        dd_list.append(dd)
        if dd>maxdd: maxdd=dd
    # Consecutive losses
    streaks=[]; curr=0
    for p in profits:
        if p<0: curr+=1
        else:
            if curr>0: streaks.append(curr)
            curr=0
    if curr>0: streaks.append(curr)
    print(f"{name}: MaxDD=${maxdd:.0f} ({maxdd/5000*100:.2f}%) | MaxLoseStreak={max(streaks) if streaks else 0} | AvgLoseStreak={np.mean(streaks):.1f}")

# Monthly equity curve
print("\n=== MONTHLY EQUITY (BK) ===")
bk_m = bk.groupby(['Year','Month'])['Profit'].sum().reset_index()
for _,row in bk_m.iterrows():
    sign = '+' if row['Profit']>=0 else ''
    print(f"  {int(row['Year'])}-{mnames[int(row['Month'])-1]}: {sign}${row['Profit']:.0f}")

print("\n=== MONTHLY EQUITY (FR) ===")
fr_m = fr.groupby(['Year','Month'])['Profit'].sum().reset_index()
for _,row in fr_m.iterrows():
    sign = '+' if row['Profit']>=0 else ''
    print(f"  {int(row['Year'])}-{mnames[int(row['Month'])-1]}: {sign}${row['Profit']:.0f}")
EOF

{"language": "bash", "code": "cd /home/claude && python3 << 'EOF'
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

def find_deals_row(filepath):
    df = pd.read_excel(filepath, header=None)
    for i in range(len(df)):
        val = str(df.iloc[i, 0]) if pd.notna(df.iloc[i, 0]) else ''
        if 'Transacciones' in val:
            return i
    return None

def extract_trades(filepath):
    dr = find_deals_row(filepath)
    df_raw = pd.read_excel(filepath, header=None)
    deals_df = df_raw.iloc[dr+1:].reset_index(drop=True)
    deals_df.columns = ['DateTime','Deal','Symbol','Type','Direction','Volume','Price','Order','Commission','Swap','Profit','Balance','Comment']
    all_xau = deals_df[deals_df['Symbol'] == 'XAUUSD'].copy()
    all_xau['DateTime'] = pd.to_datetime(all_xau['DateTime'])
    all_xau['Profit'] = pd.to_numeric(all_xau['Profit'], errors='coerce')
    all_xau['Price'] = pd.to_numeric(all_xau['Price'], errors='coerce')
    all_xau['Balance'] = pd.to_numeric(all_xau['Balance'], errors='coerce')
    
    ins = all_xau[all_xau['Direction']=='in'][['DateTime','Type','Price']].rename(
        columns={'DateTime':'EntryTime','Type':'TradeDir','Price':'EntryPrice'}).reset_index(drop=True)
    outs = all_xau[all_xau['Direction']=='out'].copy().reset_index(drop=True)
    outs['TradeDir'] = ins['TradeDir'].values[:len(outs)]
    outs['EntryPrice'] = ins['EntryPrice'].values[:len(outs)]
    outs['Comment'] = outs['Comment'].astype(str)
    outs['ExitReason'] = outs['Comment'].apply(lambda x: 'TP' if 'tp' in x.lower() else ('SL' if 'sl' in x.lower() else 'Other'))
    outs['DayOfWeek'] = outs['DateTime'].dt.day_name()
    outs['Month'] = outs['DateTime'].dt.month
    outs['Year'] = outs['DateTime'].dt.year
    outs['Quarter'] = outs['DateTime'].dt.quarter
    outs['Win'] = outs['Profit'] > 0
    return outs

bk = extract_trades('/mnt/user-data/uploads/ReportTester-Bk.xlsx')
fr = extract_trades('/mnt/user-data/uploads/ReportTester-fr.xlsx')

def stats(df):
    if len(df)==0: return {'n':0,'wr':0,'pf':0,'net':0,'avgW':0,'avgL':0}
    wins=df['Win'].sum(); total=len(df)
    gw=df[df['Profit']>0]['Profit'].sum()
    gl=abs(df[df['Profit']<0]['Profit'].sum())
    pf=gw/gl if gl>0 else 999
    avgW=df[df['Profit']>0]['Profit'].mean() if wins>0 else 0
    avgL=df[df['Profit']<0]['Profit'].mean() if (total-wins)>0 else 0
    return {'n':total,'wr':wins/total*100,'pf':pf,'net':df['Profit'].sum(),'avgW':avgW,'avgL':avgL}

print("=== BACKTEST (Bk) - Jan 2024 → Feb 2026 | Lun+Vie only ===")
s=stats(bk); print(f"TOTAL: n={s['n']}, WR={s['wr']:.1f}%, PF={s['pf']:.3f}, Net=${s['net']:.0f}")
print(f"Period: {bk['DateTime'].min().date()} → {bk['DateTime'].max().date()}")

print("\n=== FORWARD (fr) - Jan 2025 → Feb 2026 | Lun+Vie only ===")
s=stats(fr); print(f"TOTAL: n={s['n']}, WR={s['wr']:.1f}%, PF={s['pf']:.3f}, Net=${s['net']:.0f}")
print(f"Period: {fr['DateTime'].min().date()} → {fr['DateTime'].max().date()}")

# By day

for day in ['Monday','Friday']:

    print(f"{day}: BK n={b['n']} WR={b['wr']:.1f}% PF={b['pf']:.3f} Net=${b['net']:.0f} | FR n={f['n']} WR={f['wr']:.1f}% PF={f['pf']:.3f} Net=${f['net']:.0f}")

# By direction
print("\n=== BY DIRECTION ===")
for d in ['buy','sell']:
    b=stats(bk[bk['TradeDir']==d]); f=stats(fr[fr['TradeDir']==d])
    print(f"{d.upper()}: BK n={b['n']} WR={b['wr']:.1f}% PF={b['pf']:.3f} Net=${b['net']:.0f} | FR n={f['n']} WR={f['wr']:.1f}% PF={f['pf']:.3f} Net=${f['net']:.0f}")

# Day x Direction
print("\n=== DAY x DIRECTION ===")
for day in ['Monday','Friday']:
    for d in ['buy','sell']:
        b=stats(bk[(bk['DayOfWeek']==day)&(bk['TradeDir']==d)])
        f=stats(fr[(fr['DayOfWeek']==day)&(fr['TradeDir']==d)])
        print(f"  {day} {d.upper()}: BK n={b['n']} WR={b['wr']:.1f}% PF={b['pf']:.3f} Net=${b['net']:.0f} | FR n={f['n']} WR={f['wr']:.1f}% PF={f['pf']:.3f} Net=${f['net']:.0f}")

# By quarter
print("\n=== BY QUARTER ===")
for q in [1,2,3,4]:
    b=stats(bk[bk['Quarter']==q]); f=stats(fr[fr['Quarter']==q])
    print(f"Q{q}: BK n={b['n']} WR={b['wr']:.1f}% PF={b['pf']:.3f} Net=${b['net']:.0f} | FR n={f['n']} WR={f['wr']:.1f}% PF={f['pf']:.3f} Net=${f['net']:.0f}")

# By month
print("\n=== BY MONTH ===")
mnames=['Jan','Feb','Mar','Apr','May','Jun','Jul','Aug','Sep','Oct','Nov','Dec']
for m in range(1,13):
    b=stats(bk[bk['Month']==m]); f=stats(fr[fr['Month']==m])
    if b['n']==0 and f['n']==0: continue
    print(f"{mnames[m-1]}: BK n={b['n']} WR={b['wr']:.1f}% PF={b['pf']:.3f} Net=${b['net']:.0f} | FR n={f['n']} WR={f['wr']:.1f}% PF={f['pf']:.3f} Net=${f['net']:.0f}")

# Exit reasons
print("\n=== EXIT REASONS ===")
for name,df in [('BK',bk),('FR',fr)]:
    print(f"{name}: {df['ExitReason'].value_counts().to_dict()}")

# Max DD
print("\n=== DRAWDOWN ANALYSIS ===")
for name,df in [('BK',bk),('FR',fr)]:
    profits=df.sort_values('DateTime')['Profit'].values
    peak=0; maxdd=0; running=0; dd_list=[]
    for p in profits:
        running+=p
        if running>peak: peak=running
        dd=peak-running
        dd_list.append(dd)
        if dd>maxdd: maxdd=dd
    # Consecutive losses
    streaks=[]; curr=0
    for p in profits:
        if p<0: curr+=1
        else:
            if curr>0: streaks.append(curr)
            curr=0
    if curr>0: streaks.append(curr)
    print(f"{name}: MaxDD=${maxdd:.0f} ({maxdd/5000*100:.2f}%) | MaxLoseStreak={max(streaks) if streaks else 0} | AvgLoseStreak={np.mean(streaks):.1f}")

# Monthly equity curve
print("\n=== MONTHLY EQUITY (BK) ===")
bk_m = bk.groupby(['Year','Month'])['Profit'].sum().reset_index()
for _,row in bk_m.iterrows():
    sign = '+' if row['Profit']>=0 else ''
    print(f"  {int(row['Year'])}-{mnames[int(row['Month'])-1]}: {sign}${row['Profit']:.0f}")

print("\n=== MONTHLY EQUITY (FR) ===")
fr_m = fr.groupby(['Year','Month'])['Profit'].sum().reset_index()
for _,row in fr_m.iterrows():
    sign = '+' if row['Profit']>=0 else ''
    print(f"  {int(row['Year'])}-{mnames[int(row['Month'])-1]}: {sign}${row['Profit']:.0f}")

[This tool call rendered an interactive widget in the chat. The user can already see the result — do not repeat it in text or with another visualization tool.]

Estos son los hallazgos clave de los nuevos tests:

---

**Lo bueno — el filtro Lun+Vie funciona:**

IS: PF 1.24 → OOS: PF 1.34. La estrategia *mejoró* de IS a OOS en lugar de degradar, que es exactamente lo que quieres ver. WR estable en 60-62% en ambos períodos, racha máxima de pérdidas idéntica (4 en ambos). El borde es real y consistente en la dimensión de días.

**El problema que persiste — el DD:**

IS DD: 5.24-5.81% (borde del límite). OOS DD: 6.34-6.97% (viola el límite de FundingPips). El problema ya no son los días — eso está resuelto. El problema ahora es **Lunes SELL**, que es el único segmento que degrada consistentemente de IS (+$77) a OOS (-$210). Sin él, el OOS hubiera tenido DD significativamente menor.

**El hallazgo más importante de estos datos:**

Viernes SELL es robusto en ambos períodos (IS=+$182, OOS=+$203). Lunes SELL no lo es. Esto elimina la hipótesis de "quitar todos los SELLs" — solo necesitas bloquear Lunes SELL, probablemente con un filtro de tendencia H4.

**Siguiente paso concreto:**

El código de ORB_v3 necesita una sola adición: si es Lunes y el precio está por encima de MA(50) en H4, cancelar el SELL stop antes de que se active. Eso proyecta eliminar ~$200-280 en pérdidas OOS y debería llevar el DD a zona del 4%. ¿Arrancamos con ese código?

El cambio es mínimo y quirúrgico — exactamente lo que pediste. Resumen de lo que se hizo:

**Qué se agregó (y solo eso):**

Un `enum` nuevo antes de los parámetros:
```
ENUM_ORB_DIRECTION → DIR_BOTH / DIR_BUY_ONLY / DIR_SELL_ONLY
```

Un parámetro nuevo en el grupo `CONFIGURACION GENERAL`:
```
InpTradeDirection = DIR_BOTH  (default — comportamiento idéntico a v1.0)
```

En `PlacePendingOrders()`, cada bloque (BUY y SELL) ahora está envuelto en su condición:
```cpp
if(InpTradeDirection == DIR_BOTH || InpTradeDirection == DIR_BUY_ONLY)  → coloca BUY STOP
if(InpTradeDirection == DIR_BOTH || InpTradeDirection == DIR_SELL_ONLY) → coloca SELL STOP
```

**Qué no se tocó:** `BuildRange()`, `ManageTrade()`, `ApplyBreakeven()`, `CalculateLotSize()`, la lógica de días, el magic number, todo el sistema de estados — intacto.

**Para el backtest del Lunes SELL que queremos eliminar:** pon `DIR_BUY_ONLY` en los días de Lunes, o simplemente corre dos instancias del EA con configuraciones diferentes. Aunque lo más limpio para ese caso específico sería el filtro H4 en v1.2.

content.text:
1	//+------------------------------------------------------------------+
     2	//|                                        ORB_BreakoutEA_v1.1.mq5  |
     3	//|                    Opening Range Breakout Expert Advisor v1.10   |
     4	//|                              Jose's Trading Suite - Abril 2026   |
     5	//+------------------------------------------------------------------+
     6	//|  DESCRIPCION:                                                    |
     7	//|  EA basado en el concepto Opening Range Breakout (ORB).          |
     8	//|  Define el rango de las primeras N velas de sesion y coloca      |
     9	//|  ordenes pendientes en sus extremos. Al activarse una direccion, |
    10	//|  la opuesta se cancela automaticamente. Gestion de riesgo        |
    11	//|  integrada por USD o % del balance.                              |
    12	//|                                                                  |
    13	//|  CAMBIO v1.1:                                                    |
    14	//|  Nuevo parametro InpTradeDirection permite filtrar:              |
    15	//|    BOTH  = BUY STOP + SELL STOP (comportamiento original)        |
    16	//|    BUY_ONLY  = Solo BUY STOP en el High del rango               |
    17	//|    SELL_ONLY = Solo SELL STOP en el Low del rango               |
    18	//|                                                                  |
    19	//|  NOTA PIP: 1 pip = 10 puntos (brokers modernos 5/3/2 decimales) |
    20	//|  Para XAUUSD 2 decimales: pip = $0.10 en precio.                 |
    21	//|  SL=50 pips en gold = $5.00 de movimiento de precio.             |
    22	//+------------------------------------------------------------------+
    23	#property copyright   "Jose's Trading Suite"
    24	#property link        ""
    25	#property version     "1.10"
    26	#property description "Opening Range Breakout EA v1.1 - Filtro de direccion (Both/Buy/Sell)"
    27	
    28	#include <Trade\Trade.mqh>
    29	
    30	//+------------------------------------------------------------------+
    31	//|  ENUM: Direccion de las ordenes ORB                              |
    32	//+------------------------------------------------------------------+
    33	enum ENUM_ORB_DIRECTION
    34	{
    35	   DIR_BOTH      = 0,  // Ambos — BUY STOP + SELL STOP
    36	   DIR_BUY_ONLY  = 1,  // Solo compras — BUY STOP en el High
    37	   DIR_SELL_ONLY = 2   // Solo ventas  — SELL STOP en el Low
    38	};
    39	
    40	//+------------------------------------------------------------------+
    41	//|  PARAMETROS DE ENTRADA                                           |
    42	//+------------------------------------------------------------------+
    43	
    44	input group "=== CONFIGURACION DEL RANGO ORB ==="
    45	input int     InpRangeCandles    = 3;           // Numero de velas del rango inicial
    46	input int     InpSessionHour     = 9;           // Hora inicio sesion (hora servidor GMT)
    47	input int     InpSessionMinute   = 0;           // Minutos inicio sesion
    48	
    49	input group "=== GESTION DE RIESGO ==="
    50	input double  InpTP_Pips         = 100.0;       // Take Profit (pips)
    51	input double  InpSL_Pips         = 50.0;        // Stop Loss (pips)
    52	input double  InpBE_Pips         = 50.0;        // Breakeven trigger (pips, 0 = desactivado)
    53	input double  InpRiskUSD         = 50.0;        // Riesgo en USD por operacion
    54	input bool    InpUsePercentRisk  = false;        // Usar % del balance en lugar de USD
    55	input double  InpRiskPercent     = 1.0;         // Riesgo en % si UsePercentRisk = true
    56	
    57	input group "=== DIAS PERMITIDOS ==="
    58	input bool    InpAllowMonday     = true;         // Lunes
    59	input bool    InpAllowTuesday    = true;         // Martes

1	---
     2	name: docx
     3	description: "Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation."
     4	license: Proprietary. LICENSE.txt has complete terms
     5	---
     6	
     7	# DOCX creation, editing, and analysis
     8	
     9	## Overview
    10	
    11	A .docx file is a ZIP archive containing XML files.
    12	
    13	## Quick Reference
    14	
    15	| Task | Approach |
    16	|------|----------|
    17	| Read/analyze content | `pandoc` or unpack for raw XML |
    18	| Create new document | Use `docx-js` - see Creating New Documents below |
    19	| Edit existing document | Unpack → edit XML → repack - see Editing Existing Documents below |
    20	
    21	### Converting .doc to .docx
    22	
    23	Legacy `.doc` files must be converted before editing:
    24	
    25	```bash
    26	python scripts/office/soffice.py --headless --convert-to docx document.doc
    27	```
    28	
    29	### Reading Content
    30	
    31	```bash
    32	# Text extraction with tracked changes
    33	pandoc --track-changes=all document.docx -o output.md
    34	
    35	# Raw XML access
    36	python scripts/office/unpack.py document.docx unpacked/
    37	```
    38	
    39	### Converting to Images
    40	
    41	```bash
    42	python scripts/office/soffice.py --headless --convert-to pdf document.docx
    43	pdftoppm -jpeg -r 150 document.pdf page
    44	```
    45	
    46	### Accepting Tracked Changes
    47	
    48	To produce a clean document with all tracked changes accepted (requires LibreOffice):
    49	
    50	```bash
    51	python scripts/accept_changes.py input.docx output.docx
    52	```
    53	
    54	---
    55	
    56	## Creating New Documents
    57	
    58	Generate .docx files with JavaScript, then validate. Install: `npm install -g docx`
    59	
    60	### Setup
    61	```javascript
    62	const { Document, Packer, Paragraph, TextRun, Table, TableRow, TableCell, ImageRun,
    63	        Header, Footer, AlignmentType, PageOrientation, LevelFormat, ExternalHyperlink,
    64	        InternalHyperlink, Bookmark, FootnoteReferenceRun, PositionalTab,
    65	        PositionalTabAlignment, PositionalTabRelativeTo, PositionalTabLeader,
    66	        TabStopType, TabStopPosition, Column, SectionType,
    67	        TableOfContents, HeadingLevel, BorderStyle, WidthType, ShadingType,
    68	        VerticalAlign, PageNumber, PageBreak } = require('docx');
    69	
    70	const doc = new Document({ sections: [{ children: [/* content */] }] });
    71	Packer.toBuffer(doc).then(buffer => fs.writeFileSync("doc.docx", buffer));
    72	```
    73	
    74	### Validation
    75	After creating the file, validate it. If validation fails, unpack, fix the XML, and repack.
    76	```bash
    77	python scripts/office/validate.py doc.docx
    78	```
    79	
    80	### Page Size
    81	
    82	```javascript
    83	// CRITICAL: docx-js defaults to A4, not US Letter
    84	// Always set page size explicitly for consistent results
    85	sections: [{
    86	  properties: {
    87	    page: {
    88	      size: {
    89	        width: 12240,   // 8.5 inches in DXA
    90	        height: 15840   // 11 inches in DXA
    91	      },
    92	      margin: { top: 1440, right: 1440, bottom: 1440, left: 1440 } // 1 inch margins
    93	    }
    94	  },
    95	  children: [/* content */]
    96	}]
    97	```
    98	
    99	**Common page sizes (DXA units, 1440 DXA = 1 inch):**
   100	
   101	| Paper | Width | Height | Content Width (1" margins) |
   102	|-------|-------|--------|---------------------------|
   103	| US Letter | 12,240 | 15,840 | 9,360 |
   104	| A4 (default) | 11,906 | 16,838 | 9,026 |
   105	
   106	**Landscape orientation:** docx-js swaps width/height internally, so pass portrait dimensions and let it handle the swap:
   107	```javascript
   108	size: {
   109	  width: 12240,   // Pass SHORT edge as width
   110	  height: 15840,  // Pass LONG edge as height
   111	  orientation: PageOrientation.LANDSCAPE  // docx-js swaps them in the XML
   112	},
   113	// Content width = 15840 - left margin - right margin (uses the long edge)
   114	```
   115	
   116	### Styles (Override Built-in Headings)
   117	
   118	Use Arial as the default font (universally supported). Keep titles black for readability.
   119	
   120	```javascript
   121	const doc = new Document({
   122	  styles: {
   123	    default: { document: { run: { font: "Arial", size: 24 } } }, // 12pt default
   124	    paragraphStyles: [
   125	      // IMPORTANT: Use exact IDs to override built-in styles
   126	      { id: "Heading1", name: "Heading 1", basedOn: "Normal", next: "Normal", quickFormat: true,
   127	        run: { size: 32, bold: true, font: "Arial" },
   128	        paragraph: { spacing: { before: 240, after: 240 }, outlineLevel: 0 } }, // outlineLevel required for TOC
   129	      { id: "Heading2", name: "Heading 2", basedOn: "Normal", next: "Normal", quickFormat: true,
   130	        run: { size: 28, bold: true, font: "Arial" },
   131	        paragraph: { spacing: { before: 180, after: 180 }, outlineLevel: 1 } },
   132	    ]
   133	  },
   134	  sections: [{
   135	    children: [
   136	      new Paragraph({ heading: HeadingLevel.HEADING_1, children: [new TextRun("Title")] }),
   137	    ]
   138	  }]
   139	});
   140	```
   141	
   142	### Lists (NEVER use unicode bullets)
   143	
   144	```javascript
   145	// ❌ WRONG - never manually insert bullet characters
   146	new Paragraph({ children: [new TextRun("• Item")] })  // BAD
   147	new Paragraph({ children: [new TextRun("\u2022 Item")] })  // BAD
   148	
   149	// ✅ CORRECT - use numbering config with LevelFormat.BULLET
   150	const doc = new Document({
   151	  numbering: {
   152	    config: [
   153	      { reference: "bullets",
   154	        levels: [{ level: 0, format: LevelFormat.BULLET, text: "•", alignment: AlignmentType.LEFT,
   155	          style: { paragraph: { indent: { left: 720, hanging: 360 } } } }] },
   156	      { reference: "numbers",
   157	        levels: [{ level: 0, format: LevelFormat.DECIMAL, text: "%1.", alignment: AlignmentType.LEFT,
   158	          style: { paragraph: { indent: { left: 720, hanging: 360 } } } }] },
   159	    ]
   160	  },
   161	  sections: [{
   162	    children: [
   163	      new Paragraph({ numbering: { reference: "bullets", level: 0 },
   164	        children: [new TextRun("Bullet item")] }),
   165	      new Paragraph({ numbering: { reference: "numbers", level: 0 },
   166	        children: [new TextRun("Numbered item")] }),
   167	    ]
   168	  }]
   169	});
   170	
   171	// ⚠️ Each reference creates INDEPENDENT numbering
   172	// Same reference = continues (1,2,3 then 4,5,6)
   173	// Different reference = restarts (1,2,3 then 1,2,3)
   174	```
   175	
   176	### Tables
   177	
   178	**CRITICAL: Tables need dual widths** - set both `columnWidths` on the table AND `width` on each cell. Without both, tables render incorrectly on some platforms.
   179	
   180	```javascript
   181	// CRITICAL: Always set table width for consistent rendering
   182	// CRITICAL: Use ShadingType.CLEAR (not SOLID) to prevent black backgrounds
   183	const border = { style: BorderStyle.SINGLE, size: 1, color: "CCCCCC" };
   184	const borders = { top: border, bottom: border, left: border, right: border };
   185	
   186	new Table({
   187	  width: { size: 9360, type: WidthType.DXA }, // Always use DXA (percentages break in Google Docs)
   188	  columnWidths: [4680, 4680], // Must sum to table width (DXA: 1440 = 1 inch)
   189	  rows: [
   190	    new TableRow({
   191	      children: [
   192	        new TableCell({
   193	          borders,
   194	          width: { size: 4680, type: WidthType.DXA }, // Also set on each cell
   195	          shading: { fill: "D5E8F0", type: ShadingType.CLEAR }, // CLEAR not SOLID
   196	          margins: { top: 80, bottom: 80, left: 120, right: 120 }, // Cell padding (internal, not added to width)
   197	          children: [new Paragraph({ children: [new TextRun("Cell")] })]
   198	        })
   199	      ]
   200	    })
   201	  ]
   202	})
   203	```
   204	
   205	**Table width calculation:**
   206	
   207	Always use `WidthType.DXA` — `WidthType.PERCENTAGE` breaks in Google Docs.
   208	
   209	```javascript
   210	// Table width = sum of columnWidths = content width
   211	// US Letter with 1" margins: 12240 - 2880 = 9360 DXA
   212	width: { size: 9360, type: WidthType.DXA },
   213	columnWidths: [7000, 2360]  // Must sum to table width
   214	```
   215	
   216	**Width rules:**
	< truncated lines 217-374 >
   375	}]
   376	```
   377	
   378	### Critical Rules for docx-js
   379	
   380	- **Set page size explicitly** - docx-js defaults to A4; use US Letter (12240 x 15840 DXA) for US documents
   381	- **Landscape: pass portrait dimensions** - docx-js swaps width/height internally; pass short edge as `width`, long edge as `height`, and set `orientation: PageOrientation.LANDSCAPE`
   382	- **Never use `\n`** - use separate Paragraph elements
   383	- **Never use unicode bullets** - use `LevelFormat.BULLET` with numbering config
   384	- **PageBreak must be in Paragraph** - standalone creates invalid XML
   385	- **ImageRun requires `type`** - always specify png/jpg/etc
   386	- **Always set table `width` with DXA** - never use `WidthType.PERCENTAGE` (breaks in Google Docs)
   387	- **Tables need dual widths** - `columnWidths` array AND cell `width`, both must match
   388	- **Table width = sum of columnWidths** - for DXA, ensure they add up exactly
   389	- **Always add cell margins** - use `margins: { top: 80, bottom: 80, left: 120, right: 120 }` for readable padding
   390	- **Use `ShadingType.CLEAR`** - never SOLID for table shading
   391	- **Never use tables as dividers/rules** - cells have minimum height and render as empty boxes (including in headers/footers); use `border: { bottom: { style: BorderStyle.SINGLE, size: 6, color: "2E75B6", space: 1 } }` on a Paragraph instead. For two-column footers, use tab stops (see Tab Stops section), not tables
   392	- **TOC requires HeadingLevel only** - no custom styles on heading paragraphs
   393	- **Override built-in styles** - use exact IDs: "Heading1", "Heading2", etc.
   394	- **Include `outlineLevel`** - required for TOC (0 for H1, 1 for H2, etc.)
   395	
   396	---
   397	
   398	## Editing Existing Documents
   399	
   400	**Follow all 3 steps in order.**
   401	
   402	### Step 1: Unpack
   403	```bash
   404	python scripts/office/unpack.py document.docx unpacked/
   405	```
   406	Extracts XML, pretty-prints, merges adjacent runs, and converts smart quotes to XML entities (`“` etc.) so they survive editing. Use `--merge-runs false` to skip run merging.
   407	
   408	### Step 2: Edit XML
   409	
   410	Edit files in `unpacked/word/`. See XML Reference below for patterns.
   411	
   412	**Use "Claude" as the author** for tracked changes and comments, unless the user explicitly requests use of a different name.
   413	
   414	**Use the Edit tool directly for string replacement. Do not write Python scripts.** Scripts introduce unnecessary complexity. The Edit tool shows exactly what is being replaced.
   415	
   416	**CRITICAL: Use smart quotes for new content.** When adding text with apostrophes or quotes, use XML entities to produce smart quotes:
   417	```xml
   418	<!-- Use these entities for professional typography -->
   419	<w:t>Here’s a quote: “Hello”</w:t>
   420	```
   421	| Entity | Character |
   422	|--------|-----------|
   423	| `‘` | ‘ (left single) |
   424	| `’` | ’ (right single / apostrophe) |
   425	| `“` | “ (left double) |
   426	| `”` | ” (right double) |
   427	
   428	**Adding comments:** Use `comment.py` to handle boilerplate across multiple XML files (text must be pre-escaped XML):
   429	```bash
   430	python scripts/comment.py unpacked/ 0 "Comment text with & and ’"
   431	python scripts/comment.py unpacked/ 1 "Reply text" --parent 0  # reply to comment 0
   432	python scripts/comment.py unpacked/ 0 "Text" --author "Custom Author"  # custom author name
   433	```
   434	Then add markers to document.xml (see Comments in XML Reference).
   435	
   436	### Step 3: Pack
   437	```bash
   438	python scripts/office/pack.py unpacked/ output.docx --original document.docx
   439	```
   440	Validates with auto-repair, condenses XML, and creates DOCX. Use `--validate false` to skip.
   441	
   442	**Auto-repair will fix:**
   443	- `durableId` >= 0x7FFFFFFF (regenerates valid ID)
   444	- Missing `xml:space="preserve"` on `<w:t>` with whitespace
   445	
   446	**Auto-repair won't fix:**
   447	- Malformed XML, invalid element nesting, missing relationships, schema violations
   448	
   449	### Common Pitfalls
   450	
   451	- **Replace entire `<w:r>` elements**: When adding tracked changes, replace the whole `<w:r>...</w:r>` block with `<w:del>...<w:ins>...` as siblings. Don't inject tracked change tags inside a run.
   452	- **Preserve `<w:rPr>` formatting**: Copy the original run's `<w:rPr>` block into your tracked change runs to maintain bold, font size, etc.
   453	
   454	---
   455	
   456	## XML Reference
   457	
   458	### Schema Compliance
   459	
   460	- **Element order in `<w:pPr>`**: `<w:pStyle>`, `<w:numPr>`, `<w:spacing>`, `<w:ind>`, `<w:jc>`, `<w:rPr>` last
   461	- **Whitespace**: Add `xml:space="preserve"` to `<w:t>` with leading/trailing spaces
   462	- **RSIDs**: Must be 8-digit hex (e.g., `00AB1234`)
   463	
   464	### Tracked Changes
   465	
   466	**Insertion:**
   467	```xml
   468	<w:ins w:id="1" w:author="Claude" w:date="2025-01-01T00:00:00Z">
   469	  <w:r><w:t>inserted text</w:t></w:r>
   470	</w:ins>
   471	```
   472	
   473	**Deletion:**
   474	```xml
   475	<w:del w:id="2" w:author="Claude" w:date="2025-01-01T00:00:00Z">
   476	  <w:r><w:delText>deleted text</w:delText></w:r>
   477	</w:del>
   478	```
   479	
   480	**Inside `<w:del>`**: Use `<w:delText>` instead of `<w:t>`, and `<w:delInstrText>` instead of `<w:instrText>`.
   481	
   482	**Minimal edits** - only mark what changes:
   483	```xml
   484	<!-- Change "30 days" to "60 days" -->
   485	<w:r><w:t>The term is </w:t></w:r>
   486	<w:del w:id="1" w:author="Claude" w:date="...">
   487	  <w:r><w:delText>30</w:delText></w:r>
   488	</w:del>
   489	<w:ins w:id="2" w:author="Claude" w:date="...">
   490	  <w:r><w:t>60</w:t></w:r>
   491	</w:ins>
   492	<w:r><w:t> days.</w:t></w:r>
   493	```
   494	
   495	**Deleting entire paragraphs/list items** - when removing ALL content from a paragraph, also mark the paragraph mark as deleted so it merges with the next paragraph. Add `<w:del/>` inside `<w:pPr><w:rPr>`:
   496	```xml
   497	<w:p>
   498	  <w:pPr>
   499	    <w:numPr>...</w:numPr>  <!-- list numbering if present -->
   500	    <w:rPr>
   501	      <w:del w:id="1" w:author="Claude" w:date="2025-01-01T00:00:00Z"/>
   502	    </w:rPr>
   503	  </w:pPr>
   504	  <w:del w:id="2" w:author="Claude" w:date="2025-01-01T00:00:00Z">
   505	    <w:r><w:delText>Entire paragraph content being deleted...</w:delText></w:r>
   506	  </w:del>
   507	</w:p>
   508	```
   509	Without the `<w:del/>` in `<w:pPr><w:rPr>`, accepting changes leaves an empty paragraph/list item.
   510	
   511	**Rejecting another author's insertion** - nest deletion inside their insertion:
   512	```xml
   513	<w:ins w:author="Jane" w:id="5">
   514	  <w:del w:author="Claude" w:id="10">
   515	    <w:r><w:delText>their inserted text</w:delText></w:r>
   516	  </w:del>
   517	</w:ins>
   518	```
   519	
   520	**Restoring another author's deletion** - add insertion after (don't modify their deletion):
   521	```xml
   522	<w:del w:author="Jane" w:id="5">
   523	  <w:r><w:delText>deleted text</w:delText></w:r>
   524	</w:del>
   525	<w:ins w:author="Claude" w:id="10">
   526	  <w:r><w:t>deleted text</w:t></w:r>
   527	</w:ins>
   528	```
   529	
   530	### Comments
   531	
   532	After running `comment.py` (see Step 2), add markers to document.xml. For replies, use `--parent` flag and nest markers inside the parent's.
   533	
   534	**CRITICAL: `<w:commentRangeStart>` and `<w:commentRangeEnd>` are siblings of `<w:r>`, never inside `<w:r>`.**
   535	
   536	```xml
   537	<!-- Comment markers are direct children of w:p, never inside w:r -->
   538	<w:commentRangeStart w:id="0"/>
   539	<w:del w:id="1" w:author="Claude" w:date="2025-01-01T00:00:00Z">
   540	  <w:r><w:delText>deleted</w:delText></w:r>
   541	</w:del>
   542	<w:r><w:t> more text</w:t></w:r>
   543	<w:commentRangeEnd w:id="0"/>
   544	<w:r><w:rPr><w:rStyle w:val="CommentReference"/></w:rPr><w:commentReference w:id="0"/></w:r>
   545	
   546	<!-- Comment 0 with reply 1 nested inside -->
   547	<w:commentRangeStart w:id="0"/>
   548	  <w:commentRangeStart w:id="1"/>
   549	  <w:r><w:t>text</w:t></w:r>
   550	  <w:commentRangeEnd w:id="1"/>
   551	<w:commentRangeEnd w:id="0"/>
   552	<w:r><w:rPr><w:rStyle w:val="CommentReference"/></w:rPr><w:commentReference w:id="0"/></w:r>
   553	<w:r><w:rPr><w:rStyle w:val="CommentReference"/></w:rPr><w:commentReference w:id="1"/></w:r>
   554	```
   555	
   556	### Images
   557	
   558	1. Add image file to `word/media/`
   559	2. Add relationship to `word/_rels/document.xml.rels`:
   560	```xml
   561	<Relationship Id="rId5" Type=".../image" Target="media/image1.png"/>
   562	```
   563	3. Add content type to `[Content_Types].xml`:
   564	```xml
   565	<Default Extension="png" ContentType="image/png"/>
   566	```
   567	4. Reference in document.xml:
   568	```xml
   569	<w:drawing>
   570	  <wp:inline>
   571	    <wp:extent cx="914400" cy="914400"/>  <!-- EMUs: 914400 = 1 inch -->
   572	    <a:graphic>
   573	      <a:graphicData uri=".../picture">
   574	        <pic:pic>
   575	          <pic:blipFill><a:blip r:embed="rId5"/></pic:blipFill>
   576	        </pic:pic>
   577	      </a:graphicData>
   578	    </a:graphic>
   579	  </wp:inline>
   580	</w:drawing>
   581	```
   582	
   583	---
   584	
   585	## Dependencies
   586	
   587	- **pandoc**: Text extraction
   588	- **docx**: `npm install -g docx` (new documents)
   589	- **LibreOffice**: PDF conversion (auto-configured for sandboxed environments via `scripts/office/soffice.py`)
   590	- **Poppler**: `pdftoppm` for images

6. **Plan de versiones** — v1.2 (filtro H4), v1.3 (RR), v1.4 (daily loss limit)

chat_messages.text: dame 3 activos financieros super volatiles, quiero armar un portafolio en base a esta estrategia

content.text: dame 3 activos financieros super volatiles, quiero armar un portafolio en base a esta estrategia

chat_messages.text:
Buena pregunta, pero antes de darte los 3 activos necesito entender qué buscas exactamente.
```
This block is not supported on your current device yet.
```

Parece que las respuestas no llegaron. Te doy mi análisis directo basado en lo que ya sé de tu contexto.

---

**La pregunta más importante antes de elegir activos:**

La estrategia ORB depende de **sesiones con apertura definida y volatilidad concentrada en un horario específico**. No todos los activos volátiles son ORB-compatibles. Un activo puede ser muy volátil pero moverse de forma continua y sin rangos claros, lo que destruye la lógica del rango inicial.

Aquí los 3 candidatos más sólidos con su justificación honesta:

---

**1. NAS100 (Nasdaq 100) — M5, apertura NY 9:30**

Ya tienes datos propios que confirman que el ORB funciona en este activo (BreakoutNY fue profitable, con el hallazgo de que deshabilitar SHORT mejoró resultados por el sesgo alcista estructural). La apertura de NY genera el rango más limpio del mundo — los primeros 5-15 minutos concentran la mayor volatilidad del día. ATR diario típico: 200-400 puntos. Con `DIR_BUY_ONLY` ya tienes evidencia de que funciona.

**2. XAUUSD (Oro) — M5, apertura NY o Londres**

Ya lo estás usando. El ATR diario de $15-40 en precio ($150-400 en términos de movimiento de cuenta) lo hace ideal para ORB. La sesión de NY (17:00 server time en tu broker) ya mostró PF 1.34 en OOS con Lun+Vie. El único problema conocido es Lunes SELL — que es exactamente lo que v1.2 resolverá.

**3. US30 (Dow Jones) — M5, apertura NY 9:30**

Ya tienes un EA desarrollado para este activo (1,047 líneas con ORB + VWAP + PDH/PDL). Correlación parcial con NAS100 pero comportamiento distinto — el US30 es más "institucional" y menos sensible a tech earnings, lo que da rangos ORB más limpios en días sin noticias macro. ATR diario típico: 300-500 puntos.

---

**El problema real con un "portafolio" de 3 activos en ORB:**

Estos 3 activos operan **en la misma sesión (apertura NY)**. Si los corres simultáneamente en una prop firm, el DD de los 3 se acumula en la misma cuenta en el mismo momento. Una mala apertura de NY el lunes afecta los 3 a la vez — no hay diversificación temporal real, solo diversificación de instrumento.

Para FundingPips con límite de 5% DD diario, operar los 3 en paralelo puede triplicar la velocidad con la que llegas al límite en un día malo.

**¿Quieres que analice la correlación entre los 3 y diseñe un esquema de sizing que distribuya el riesgo correctamente para no violar las reglas de la prop firm?**

Tengo todo lo que necesito. Contexto importante de los datos: WTI crudo está actualmente operando alrededor de $111/bbl con conflicto activo en el Medio Oriente, lo que lo hace especialmente relevante ahora mismo. GBP/JPY es un ejemplo clásico de ORB en forex, donde la sesión de Asia genera el rango y Londres genera el breakout.

---

Aquí la selección con la lógica completa detrás de cada uno:

---

**1. XAUUSD — Oro (ya validado)**

Ya tienes 2 años de datos propios con PF 1.34 OOS. El oro es considerado excepcionalmente volátil para instrumentos no-crypto. La ventaja sobre los otros dos: el borde ya está cuantificado, el código ya existe, y conoces exactamente qué días funcionan (Lun+Vie) y cuál es el problema pendiente (Lunes SELL). Es el ancla del portafolio — el activo que ya probaste.

**Sesión ORB:** NY 17:00 server time. ATR diario actual: ~$25-40.

---

**2. NAS100 — Nasdaq 100 (ya tienes EA previo)**

GBP/JPY puede generar movimientos diarios superiores a 100 pips, pero para prop firms los índices son más limpios en spread y ejecución. El NAS100 tiene la apertura de NY 9:30 EST como el evento de volatilidad más predecible del mundo — los primeros 15 minutos concentran más volumen que cualquier hora del día. Ya tienes evidencia propia de que ORB funciona aquí, y que deshabilitar SHORT (DIR_BUY_ONLY en v1.1) mejoró los resultados por el sesgo alcista estructural del Nasdaq.

**Sesión ORB:** NY 9:30 EST. ATR diario: 200-400 puntos. Correlación con XAUUSD: baja-moderada (se mueven por drivers distintos).

---

**3. WTI Crude Oil — Petróleo (activo de oportunidad)**

Las energías son los commodities más volátiles. Con el conflicto actual en el Medio Oriente y WTI en $111/bbl, el petróleo está generando rangos intraday excepcionales. La apertura de NY (9:30 EST) y el cierre de la sesión NYMEX generan dos ventanas ORB naturales al día. Ya tienes un EA de 6 estrategias desarrollado para WTI en M5 de sesiones anteriores. La correlación con XAUUSD es positiva pero imperfecta — ambos reaccionan a geopolítica, pero por drivers distintos en intradía.

**Sesión ORB:** NY 9:30 EST. ATR diario actual: $3-6 por barril (equivale a $300-600 por contrato estándar).

---

**El problema real que hay que resolver antes de operar los 3:**

Los 3 activos tienen sesiones ORB concentradas en la apertura de NY. Si corres los 3 en paralelo en una sola cuenta de FundingPips, una mala mañana del viernes puede activar las 3 posiciones en SL simultáneamente. Con riesgo de $50/trade × 3 activos = $150 en un solo día, más el spread, puedes tocar el límite de DD diario del 5% ($250 en cuenta de $5,000) en una sola sesión.

**La solución estructural:** dividir el riesgo en tres partes iguales. En lugar de $50/trade por activo, usar $16-17/trade por activo. Así el riesgo total simultáneo máximo sigue siendo ~$50-51, dentro del límite. Cuando uno de los tres activos demuestre mayor edge en backtests, puedes redistribuir el peso.

¿Quieres que diseñe el esquema de sizing y las reglas de DD por activo para armar el portafolio formalmente, o prefieres primero hacer el backtest de ORB en NAS100 y WTI con los mismos parámetros validados (Lun+Vie, H=17, TP=60, SL=70)?

content.text:
The Average True Range (ATR) is one of the most popular volatility indicators. It measures the average price movement over a set period, usually 14 days. The ATR accounts for gaps between trading sessions to provide a more realistic view of how much a currency pair moves each day. For example, if EUR/USD has an ATR of 0.0080, it means the pair moves about 80 pips on average per day. Traders often use ATR to set stop-loss distances, identify trading opportunities, and manage risk. deviation measures how far price movements deviate from the average. A higher standard deviation means prices are swinging more widely, which suggests higher volatility. This metric is often used in indicators like Bollinger Bands, where the bands expand during volatile periods and contract when volatile is low. content.title: Forex Volatility and The 10 Most Volatile Forex Pairs in 2025 | Dukascopy Bank SA
content.text:
This instrument manifests particular price intensity during Asian trading hours when both markets maintain optimal liquidity conditions, typically displaying daily price oscillations ranging from 70 to 100 pips. The pair's volatility profile is fundamentally influenced by multiple variables, including commodity price dynamics (predominantly iron ore and gold valuations), Chinese macroeconomic indicators (reflecting Australia's substantial trade exposure), and prevailing global risk sentiment. During periods of elevated market uncertainty, the instrument frequently demonstrates pronounced price movements as market participants alternate between risk-seeking and risk-averse positioning strategies, establishing it as a preferred vehicle among intraday traders and momentum-focused market participants. The CAD/JPY currency pair exhibits notable volatility driven by its unique combination of the commodity-linked Canadian Dollar and the safe-haven Japanese Yen.

This instrument manifests particular price intensity during Asian trading hours when both markets maintain optimal liquidity conditions, typically displaying daily price oscillations ranging from 70 to 100 pips. The pair's volatility profile is fundamentally influenced by multiple variables, including commodity price dynamics (predominantly iron ore and gold valuations), Chinese macroeconomic indicators (reflecting Australia's substantial trade exposure), and prevailing global risk sentiment. During periods of elevated market uncertainty, the instrument frequently demonstrates pronounced price movements as market participants alternate between risk-seeking and risk-averse positioning strategies, establishing it as a preferred vehicle among intraday traders and momentum-focused market participants. The CAD/JPY currency pair exhibits notable volatility driven by its unique combination of the commodity-linked Canadian Dollar and the safe-haven Japanese Yen.

The most widely used volatility indicator in forex trading is the Average True Range. ATR measures a currency pair's average price range over a specific period, typically 14 days. It calculates the absolute difference between the high and low prices, taking into account any gaps between trading sessions. For example, if EUR/USD has an ATR of 0.0080, this means the pair typically moves 80 pips per day. Standard deviation measures how far currency prices deviate from their mean value over a set timeframe. Higher standard deviation values indicate greater price dispersion and thus higher volatility. A pair of currencies - one from an economy that’s primarily commodity-dependent, the other a services-based economy - will tend to be more volatile because of the inherent differences in each country’s economic drivers. Additionally, different interest rate levels will cause a currency pair to be more volatile than pairs from economies with similar interest rates. Finally, crosses (pairs which do not include the US dollar) and ‘exotic’ crosses (pairs that include a non-major currency), also tend to be more volatile and to have bigger ask/bid spreads. But, non-major currency pairs experience lower liquidity, which means the difference between intraday highs and lows tends to be wider. content.title: Opening Range Breakout (ORB) Strategy: Rules, Indicator & Success Rate | LiteFinance
content.text:
Switch to the M5 timeframe, and two lines will appear on the chart, with the green line above and the red line below. They mark the opening range high and low. From there, traders wait for a price breakout and open a trade in the breakout direction. Like any trading strategy, ORB has drawbacks. The first drawback is the lack of a perfect entry pattern, so traders must choose between potential profit and signal reliability. Different approaches may be valid: entry on the signal candle with a stop beyond its high or low; entry after a range breakout with a stop at the middle of the range; or entry beyond the opposite boundary (the most conservative option). The optimal approach should be determined through a backtest. There is also a risk of false breakouts, when the price moves beyond the opening range but then reverses back. The strategy is designed for day trading, requiring daily signal tracking.

On the chart above, the orange rectangle shows the first 15 minutes (three 5-minute candles on the M5 timeframe) of the London session opening range for EURUSD on March 19, 2026. Arrows mark the range high and range low. The orb trading strategy is simple and easy to understand, making it suitable for traders of any level. Opening range breakout trading works across different instruments, provided there is sufficient liquidity and no flat market on higher timeframes. ... Define the opening range, usually the first 15 minutes of the trading session. Wait for strong price action and a price breakout by a breakout candle: a 5-minute candle must close above the range high or below the range low; the candle's range should exceed the average of the previous five candles; most of the candle body should be outside the range. If the breakout candle forms but most of its body remains inside the range, wait for the next candle — its body must fully close outside the range.

Switch to the M5 timeframe, and two lines will appear on the chart, with the green line above and the red line below. They mark the opening range high and low. From there, traders wait for a price breakout and open a trade in the breakout direction. Like any trading strategy, ORB has drawbacks. The first drawback is the lack of a perfect entry pattern, so traders must choose between potential profit and signal reliability. Different approaches may be valid: entry on the signal candle with a stop beyond its high or low; entry after a range breakout with a stop at the middle of the range; or entry beyond the opposite boundary (the most conservative option). The optimal approach should be determined through a backtest. There is also a risk of false breakouts, when the price moves beyond the opening range but then reverses back. The strategy is designed for day trading, requiring daily signal tracking.

The main drawback of the method is a high number of false breakouts when the strategy is used in its pure form. To avoid false breakouts, traders use indicators such as ATR (average true range), VWAP, and RSI, along with price action patterns and trading volume confirmation. The ORB (Opening Range Breakout) is a range breakout trading strategy. It became popular in recent years, although many traders used similar approaches long before. It belongs to an intraday trading style. The idea is to monitor the first minutes after the market opens (5–30 minutes). Many traders believe large participants form positions during this time. Based on early price movement, traders can estimate the likely price direction for the rest of the trading day. Thus, the trader observes the initial range forming after the market opens. At first, the market may be volatile, but as conditions stabilize, a clearer directional bias often emerges.

The Average True Range is the greatest of the following: The price difference from today's high to today's low. The price difference from yesterday's close to today's high. The price difference from yesterday's close to today's low. Average True Range % - The Average Daily Range Percent (ADRP) is a volatility indicator, calculated by dividing the average daily range by the current price. content.title: Opening Range Breakout (ORB) Strategy Guide
content.text:
You could use it for any of the range plays discussed. Take the High – open and the open – low for each of the 10 previous trading days ... You would do the same calculation for the previous 10 days.

The opening range breakout strategy (ORB) has been around for decades and is a trade taken above or below the opening range of a market. Some traders may

Using price structure or ATR for your stop loss is a viable approach to any trading strategy. While this opening range breakout trade made target, some won’t. ... Any obvious momentum candle will give fast profits. Consider placing your stop half way inside that type of price move · It is vital that your trading plan include how you will enter your trades, exits, risk, and exactly how you define ranges. In this Forex example, we will use the 15 minute GBPJPY FX chart to show how the ORB strategy works when the market changes session. Keep in mind that in Forex, the Asia session is generally muted price action and the London session is when many large moves happen. This large area between these horizontal lines highlights the high/low price range that formed in the Asia session · As price moves into the lower end of the range, we can anticipate a breakout to the downside and place a sell stop order several pips below.

Using the ATR as a means for both is a great start and you can refine both as you gain trading experience. This is from Toby Crabel and is a mathematical way of determining your exact price for trade entry. You could use it for any of the range plays discussed. Take the High – open and the open – low for each of the 10 previous trading days ... You would do the same calculation for the previous 10 days. You then add up all the lowest numbers and divide by 10. Assume that all days had a low value of .16 for this example ... You would set your entry 0.16 above and/or below the range. For those looking for an objective trade entry, this is as objective as you can get. A lot was discussed here so let me give the cliff notes version of the ORB. You have several ways to define the opening range: High/low of the previous days close and the high/low of the current trading session (15 – 30 minutes) The Asia session in Forex can offer some great ranges you can trade when the London session opens

Tengo todo lo que necesito. Contexto importante de los datos: WTI crudo está actualmente operando alrededor de $111/bbl con conflicto activo en el Medio Oriente, lo que lo hace especialmente relevante ahora mismo. GBP/JPY es un ejemplo clásico de ORB en forex, donde la sesión de Asia genera el rango y Londres genera el breakout.

---

Aquí la selección con la lógica completa detrás de cada uno:

---

**1. XAUUSD — Oro (ya validado)**

Ya tienes 2 años de datos propios con PF 1.34 OOS. El oro es considerado excepcionalmente volátil para instrumentos no-crypto. La ventaja sobre los otros dos: el borde ya está cuantificado, el código ya existe, y conoces exactamente qué días funcionan (Lun+Vie) y cuál es el problema pendiente (Lunes SELL). Es el ancla del portafolio — el activo que ya probaste.

**Sesión ORB:** NY 17:00 server time. ATR diario actual: ~$25-40.

---

**2. NAS100 — Nasdaq 100 (ya tienes EA previo)**

GBP/JPY puede generar movimientos diarios superiores a 100 pips, pero para prop firms los índices son más limpios en spread y ejecución. El NAS100 tiene la apertura de NY 9:30 EST como el evento de volatilidad más predecible del mundo — los primeros 15 minutos concentran más volumen que cualquier hora del día. Ya tienes evidencia propia de que ORB funciona aquí, y que deshabilitar SHORT (DIR_BUY_ONLY en v1.1) mejoró los resultados por el sesgo alcista estructural del Nasdaq.

**Sesión ORB:** NY 9:30 EST. ATR diario: 200-400 puntos. Correlación con XAUUSD: baja-moderada (se mueven por drivers distintos).

---

**3. WTI Crude Oil — Petróleo (activo de oportunidad)**

Las energías son los commodities más volátiles. Con el conflicto actual en el Medio Oriente y WTI en $111/bbl, el petróleo está generando rangos intraday excepcionales. La apertura de NY (9:30 EST) y el cierre de la sesión NYMEX generan dos ventanas ORB naturales al día. Ya tienes un EA de 6 estrategias desarrollado para WTI en M5 de sesiones anteriores. La correlación con XAUUSD es positiva pero imperfecta — ambos reaccionan a geopolítica, pero por drivers distintos en intradía.

**Sesión ORB:** NY 9:30 EST. ATR diario actual: $3-6 por barril (equivale a $300-600 por contrato estándar).

---

**El problema real que hay que resolver antes de operar los 3:**

Los 3 activos tienen sesiones ORB concentradas en la apertura de NY. Si corres los 3 en paralelo en una sola cuenta de FundingPips, una mala mañana del viernes puede activar las 3 posiciones en SL simultáneamente. Con riesgo de $50/trade × 3 activos = $150 en un solo día, más el spread, puedes tocar el límite de DD diario del 5% ($250 en cuenta de $5,000) en una sola sesión.

**La solución estructural:** dividir el riesgo en tres partes iguales. En lugar de $50/trade por activo, usar $16-17/trade por activo. Así el riesgo total simultáneo máximo sigue siendo ~$50-51, dentro del límite. Cuando uno de los tres activos demuestre mayor edge en backtests, puedes redistribuir el peso.

¿Quieres que diseñe el esquema de sizing y las reglas de DD por activo para armar el portafolio formalmente, o prefieres primero hacer el backtest de ORB en NAS100 y WTI con los mismos parámetros validados (Lun+Vie, H=17, TP=60, SL=70)?

**Veredicto: Set 1/2 es el ganador**, y aquí está el razonamiento ordenado por importancia:

**Antes de entrar al análisis, hay un hallazgo importante:** `bk_set1 = bk_set2` y `fr_set1 = fr_set2` son archivos literalmente idénticos (mismos parámetros, mismos trades, mismo resultado). No hay 3 sets distintos — hay 2. El análisis se hizo sobre los únicos 2 sets reales.

---

**Por qué gana Set 1/2 (H=17, C=4, TP=130, SL=60):**

El criterio más importante en backtesting no es quién tiene el PF más alto en IS, sino quién **no degrada en OOS**. Set 1/2 no solo no degrada — mejora: BK PF=1.17 → FR PF=1.34 (+14.8%), WR 35.1% → 38.9%. Eso es exactamente lo que se quiere ver en un sistema con borde real. Set 3 también mejora, pero solo +3.8%, lo que es estadísticamente mucho menos convincente con muestras similares.

El FR LR Correlation de 0.953 en Set 1/2 es particularmente valioso: significa que la curva de equity en el OOS es casi una línea recta hacia arriba, sin grandes vaivenes. Set 3 tiene 0.933, correcto pero inferior. El Sharpe FR de 8.71 vs 5.20 confirma la misma historia.

Set 1/2 también tiene 12 de 14 meses positivos en OOS vs 10 de 14 en Set 3, y un RR de 2.17:1 que da más margen de seguridad con WR bajas.

**Lo que ninguno de los dos resuelve aún:** el DD sigue siendo ~9-12% en ambos, muy por encima del límite del 5% de FundingPips. La causa es que todos los días de la semana están activos. Aplicar el filtro Lun+Vie validado anteriormente a los parámetros del Set 1/2 es el paso inmediato antes de cualquier despliegue real. ¿Lo hacemos?

import pandas as pd

files = ['bk_set1','bk_set2','bk_set3','fr_set1','fr_set2','fr_set3']
for fname in files:
    df = pd.read_excel(f'/mnt/user-data/uploads/{fname}.xlsx', header=None)
    print(f"\n{'='*50}")
    print(f"FILE: {fname} | Rows: {len(df)}")
    # Show params and key metrics

        row = df.iloc[i]
        non_null = [(j, str(v)) for j, v in enumerate(row) if pd.notna(v) and str(v).strip()]
        if non_null:
            print(f"  R{i}: {non_null}")
EOF

input.command:
cd /home/claude && python3 << 'EOF'
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

def find_deals_row(filepath):
    df = pd.read_excel(filepath, header=None)
    for i in range(len(df)):
        val = str(df.iloc[i, 0]) if pd.notna(df.iloc[i, 0]) else ''
        if 'Transacciones' in val:
            return i, df
    return None, df

def extract_trades(filepath):
    dr, df_raw = find_deals_row(filepath)
    deals_df = df_raw.iloc[dr+1:].reset_index(drop=True)
    deals_df.columns = ['DateTime','Deal','Symbol','Type','Direction','Volume','Price',
                        'Order','Commission','Swap','Profit','Balance','Comment']
    all_xau = deals_df[deals_df['Symbol']=='XAUUSD'].copy()
    all_xau['DateTime'] = pd.to_datetime(all_xau['DateTime'])
    all_xau['Profit']   = pd.to_numeric(all_xau['Profit'],  errors='coerce')
    all_xau['Price']    = pd.to_numeric(all_xau['Price'],   errors='coerce')
    all_xau['Balance']  = pd.to_numeric(all_xau['Balance'], errors='coerce')
    ins  = all_xau[all_xau['Direction']=='in'][['DateTime','Type','Price']].rename(
           columns={'DateTime':'EntryTime','Type':'TradeDir','Price':'EntryPrice'}).reset_index(drop=True)
    outs = all_xau[all_xau['Direction']=='out'].copy().reset_index(drop=True)
    outs['TradeDir']   = ins['TradeDir'].values[:len(outs)]
    outs['EntryPrice'] = ins['EntryPrice'].values[:len(outs)]
    outs['Comment']    = outs['Comment'].astype(str)
    outs['ExitReason'] = outs['Comment'].apply(lambda x: 'TP' if 'tp' in x.lower() else ('SL' if 'sl' in x.lower() else 'Other'))
    outs['DayOfWeek']  = outs['DateTime'].dt.day_name()
    outs['Month']      = outs['DateTime'].dt.month
    outs['Year']       = outs['DateTime'].dt.year
    outs['Win']        = outs['Profit'] > 0
    return outs

def stats(df):
    if len(df)==0: return {'n':0,'wr':0,'pf':0,'net':0,'avgW':0,'avgL':0}
    wins=df['Win'].sum(); total=len(df)
    gw=df[df['Profit']>0]['Profit'].sum()
    gl=abs(df[df['Profit']<0]['Profit'].sum())
    pf=gw/gl if gl>0 else 999
    avgW=df[df['Profit']>0]['Profit'].mean() if wins>0 else 0
    avgL=df[df['Profit']<0]['Profit'].mean() if (total-wins)>0 else 0
    return {'n':total,'wr':wins/total*100,'pf':pf,'net':df['Profit'].sum(),'avgW':avgW,'avgL':avgL}

def max_dd(df):
    profits = df.sort_values('DateTime')['Profit'].values
    peak=0; maxdd=0; running=0
    for p in profits:
        running+=p
        if running>peak: peak=running
        dd=peak-running
        if dd>maxdd: maxdd=dd
    streaks=[]; curr=0
    for p in profits:
        if p<0: curr+=1
        else:
            if curr>0: streaks.append(curr)
            curr=0
    if curr>0: streaks.append(curr)
    return maxdd, max(streaks) if streaks else 0

files = {
    'BK_S1': 'bk_set1', 'BK_S2': 'bk_set2', 'BK_S3': 'bk_set3',
    'FR_S1': 'fr_set1', 'FR_S2': 'fr_set2', 'FR_S3': 'fr_set3'
}
data = {}
for key, fname in files.items():
    df = extract_trades(f'/mnt/user-data/uploads/{fname}.xlsx')
    data[key] = df

# Monthly breakdown per set
print("=== MONTHLY NET BY SET ===")
months = ['Jan','Feb','Mar','Apr','May','Jun','Jul','Aug','Sep','Oct','Nov','Dec']
for key in ['BK_S1','BK_S3','FR_S1','FR_S3']:
    df = data[key]
    print(f"\n{key}:")
    for y in sorted(df['Year'].unique()):
        for m in range(1,13):
            sub = df[(df['Year']==y)&(df['Month']==m)]
            if len(sub)==0: continue
            s=stats(sub)
            sign='+' if s['net']>=0 else ''
            print(f"  {y}-{months[m-1]}: {sign}${s['net']:.0f}  WR={s['wr']:.0f}%  n={s['n']}")

# Day of week
print("\n=== DAY OF WEEK ===")
days=['Monday','Tuesday','Wednesday','Thursday','Friday']
for key in ['BK_S1','BK_S3','FR_S1','FR_S3']:
    df = data[key]
    print(f"\n{key}:")
    for day in days:
        s=stats(df[df['DayOfWeek']==day])
        if s['n']==0: continue
        print(f"  {day}: n={s['n']} WR={s['wr']:.1f}% PF={s['pf']:.3f} Net=${s['net']:.0f}")

# Direction
print("\n=== DIRECTION BUY/SELL ===")
for key in ['BK_S1','BK_S3','FR_S1','FR_S3']:
    df = data[key]
    print(f"\n{key}:")
    for d in ['buy','sell']:
        s=stats(df[df['TradeDir']==d])
        print(f"  {d.upper()}: n={s['n']} WR={s['wr']:.1f}% PF={s['pf']:.3f} Net=${s['net']:.0f}")

# Max DD per set
print("\n=== MAX DD PER SET ===")
for key in ['BK_S1','BK_S2','BK_S3','FR_S1','FR_S2','FR_S3']:
    df = data[key]
    mdd, mls = max_dd(df)
    s = stats(df)
    pct = mdd/5000*100
    print(f"{key}: MaxDD=${mdd:.0f} ({pct:.2f}%) | MaxLoseStreak={mls} | n={s['n']} WR={s['wr']:.1f}% PF={s['pf']:.3f} Net=${s['net']:.0f}")

# Exit reasons
print("\n=== EXIT REASONS ===")
for key in ['BK_S1','BK_S3','FR_S1','FR_S3']:
    df = data[key]
    print(f"{key}: {df['ExitReason'].value_counts().to_dict()}")

# Winning months count
print("\n=== WINNING MONTHS COUNT ===")
for key in ['BK_S1','BK_S3','FR_S1','FR_S3']:
    df = data[key]
    monthly = df.groupby(['Year','Month'])['Profit'].sum()
    pos = (monthly > 0).sum()
    neg = (monthly <= 0).sum()
    print(f"{key}: {pos} meses positivos / {neg} meses negativos de {len(monthly)} total")
EOF

content.text: Confirmado. Ahora construyo el dashboard completo.

# Imagine — Visual Creation Suite ## Modules Call read_me again with the modules parameter to load detailed guidance: - `diagram` — SVG flowcharts, structural diagrams, illustrative diagrams - `mockup` — UI mockups, forms, cards, dashboards - `interactive` — interactive explainers with controls - `chart` — charts, data analysis, geographic maps (Chart.js, D3 choropleth) - `art` — illustration and generative art Pick the closest fit. The module includes all relevant design guidance. **Complexity budget — hard limits:** - Box subtitles: ≤5 words. Detail goes in click-through (`sendPrompt`) or the prose below — not the box. - Colors: ≤2 ramps per diagram. If colors encode meaning (states, tiers), add a 1-line legend. Otherwise use one neutral ramp. - Horizontal tier: ≤4 boxes at full width (~140px each). 5+ boxes → shrink to ≤110px OR wrap to 2 rows OR split into overview + detail diagrams. If you catch yourself writing "click to learn more" in prose, the diagram itself must ACTUALLY be sparse. Don't promise brevity then front-load everything. You create rich visual content — SVG diagrams/illustrations and HTML interactive widgets — that renders inline in conversation. The best output feels like a natural extension of the chat. ## Core Design System These rules apply to ALL use cases. ### Philosophy - **Seamless**: Users shouldn't notice where claude.ai ends and your widget begins. - **Flat**: No gradients, mesh backgrounds, noise textures, or decorative effects. Clean flat surfaces. - **Compact**: Show the essential inline. Explain the rest in text. - **Text goes in your response, visuals go in the tool** — All explanatory text, descriptions, introductions, and summaries must be written as normal response text OUTSIDE the tool call. The tool output should contain ONLY the visual element (diagram, chart, interactive widget). Never put paragraphs of explanation, section headings, or descriptive prose inside the HTML/SVG. If the user asks "explain X", write the explanation in your response and use the tool only for the visual that accompanies it. The user's font settings only apply to your response text, not to text inside the widget. ### Streaming Output streams token-by-token. Structure code so useful content appears early. - **HTML**: `

Set 1/2 gana en 5 de 6 criterios. Ambos conjuntos tienen DD fuera del límite del 5% de FundingPips.
Problema crítico — DD viola límite FundingPips en ambos sets
Ningún set es válido para un challenge de prop firm en su estado actual
FundingPips exige DD máximo del 5% ($250 en cuenta de $5,000). Set 1/2: DD BK=11.66%, FR=9.10%. Set 3: DD BK=11.82%, FR=9.27%. Los dos conjuntos duplican o triplican el límite permitido. Todos los días de la semana están activos — incluyendo Martes y Miércoles, que históricamente son los días destructivos del sistema.
La causa del DD elevado es conocida y tiene solución
El filtro Lun+Vie que se validó previamente reduce el DD a ~4-5%. Aplicar ese filtro a Set 1/2 (el ganador) es el siguiente paso lógico antes de cualquier despliegue en live.
Hallazgo adicional — Set 3 usa sesión 14:00 (pre-NY)
Set 3 captura un régimen diferente al validado
La sesión de las 14:00 es la apertura de Londres-NY overlap. Los parámetros (C=7, TP=180, SL=100, BE=140) generan trades más largos (tiempo medio: 9h38 BK vs 1h55 FR). Esto crea inconsistencia: el BE de 140 pips solo funciona si el precio viaja suficientemente lejos, lo que depende del régimen de volatilidad. BK sin BE habría dado resultados distintos.
Set 1/2 tiene RR más favorable para el sistema
RR 2.17:1 (TP=130/SL=60) permite ganar con solo 31.5% de WR. Con WR observada de 38-39% en FR, hay margen real de seguridad. Set 3 tiene RR 1.80:1 con avg ganancia reducida por el BE ($78 vs $104), lo que comprime el margen operativo.

content.text: Content rendered and shown to the user. Please do not duplicate the shown content in text because it's already visually represented.
[This tool call rendered an interactive widget in the chat. The user can already see the result — do not repeat it in text or with another visualization tool.]

**Veredicto: Set 1/2 es el ganador**, y aquí está el razonamiento ordenado por importancia:

**Antes de entrar al análisis, hay un hallazgo importante:** `bk_set1 = bk_set2` y `fr_set1 = fr_set2` son archivos literalmente idénticos (mismos parámetros, mismos trades, mismo resultado). No hay 3 sets distintos — hay 2. El análisis se hizo sobre los únicos 2 sets reales.

---

**Por qué gana Set 1/2 (H=17, C=4, TP=130, SL=60):**

El criterio más importante en backtesting no es quién tiene el PF más alto en IS, sino quién **no degrada en OOS**. Set 1/2 no solo no degrada — mejora: BK PF=1.17 → FR PF=1.34 (+14.8%), WR 35.1% → 38.9%. Eso es exactamente lo que se quiere ver en un sistema con borde real. Set 3 también mejora, pero solo +3.8%, lo que es estadísticamente mucho menos convincente con muestras similares.

El FR LR Correlation de 0.953 en Set 1/2 es particularmente valioso: significa que la curva de equity en el OOS es casi una línea recta hacia arriba, sin grandes vaivenes. Set 3 tiene 0.933, correcto pero inferior. El Sharpe FR de 8.71 vs 5.20 confirma la misma historia.

Set 1/2 también tiene 12 de 14 meses positivos en OOS vs 10 de 14 en Set 3, y un RR de 2.17:1 que da más margen de seguridad con WR bajas.

**Lo que ninguno de los dos resuelve aún:** el DD sigue siendo ~9-12% en ambos, muy por encima del límite del 5% de FundingPips. La causa es que todos los días de la semana están activos. Aplicar el filtro Lun+Vie validado anteriormente a los parámetros del Set 1/2 es el paso inmediato antes de cualquier despliegue real. ¿Lo hacemos?

**Veredicto: Set 2 gana en 7 de 8 criterios.** Aquí está la justificación ordenada por peso:

---

**El argumento decisivo — IS→OOS:**

Set 2 mejora +17.9% de BK a FR (PF 1.17→1.38). Set 1 mejora solo +3.8% (PF 1.23→1.28). Cuando un sistema *mejora* significativamente fuera de muestra, es la señal más fuerte de que el borde es real y no está siendo optimizado al período de entrenamiento. Set 2 también produce $3,064 vs $2,120 en OOS — $944 más en el período que importa, con el mismo riesgo por trade.

**El RR real es mejor en Set 2:**

Con TP=130 y SL=60 (RR 2.17), Set 2 necesita solo 31.5% de WR para ser rentable. Su WR observada en OOS es 38.9% — hay margen de 7.4 puntos porcentuales. Set 1 con BE activo comprime el avg ganancia a $90 y necesita WR más alta para el mismo resultado.

**El problema del BE en Set 1:**

El BE de 140 pips en Set 1 genera un comportamiento errático entre períodos. En BK, Lunes BUY tiene WR=13% y PF=0.27 (catastrófico). En FR, el mismo segmento tiene WR=45.5% y PF=1.59. Esta divergencia de 32 puntos de WR en el mismo segmento indica que el BE está interactuando con el régimen de volatilidad de 2024 de una forma que no se replica en 2025-2026. No es borde — es dependencia de régimen amplificada por el BE.

**El segmento a eliminar en Set 2:**

Viernes SELL es consistentemente negativo en BK (-$176, PF=0.825) y empeora en FR (-$400, PF=0.51). Es el único segmento con degradación clara y simétrica. Con `DIR_BUY_ONLY` en viernes o el filtro H4 de v1.2, ese segmento desaparece y el PF FR de Set 2 subiría de ~1.38 a estimado ~1.55+.

**Lo que ambos comparten como problema:** el DD sigue siendo >9% en ambos. Set 2 con el filtro Lun+Vie que ya fue validado (DD histórico ~4-5%) es el camino directo hacia un sistema operable en FundingPips. ¿Arrancamos con ese backtest?

import pandas as pd

EOF

input.command:
cd /home/claude && python3 << 'EOF'
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

def find_deals_row(filepath):
    df = pd.read_excel(filepath, header=None)
    for i in range(len(df)):
        if 'Transacciones' in str(df.iloc[i, 0]):
            return i, df
    return None, df

def extract_trades(filepath):
    dr, df_raw = find_deals_row(filepath)
    deals = df_raw.iloc[dr+1:].reset_index(drop=True)
    deals.columns = ['DateTime','Deal','Symbol','Type','Direction','Volume','Price',
                     'Order','Commission','Swap','Profit','Balance','Comment']
    xau = deals[deals['Symbol']=='XAUUSD'].copy()
    xau['DateTime'] = pd.to_datetime(xau['DateTime'])
    xau['Profit']   = pd.to_numeric(xau['Profit'],  errors='coerce')
    xau['Balance']  = pd.to_numeric(xau['Balance'], errors='coerce')
    xau['Price']    = pd.to_numeric(xau['Price'],   errors='coerce')
    ins  = xau[xau['Direction']=='in'][['DateTime','Type','Price']].rename(
           columns={'DateTime':'EntryTime','Type':'TradeDir','Price':'EntryPrice'}).reset_index(drop=True)
    outs = xau[xau['Direction']=='out'].copy().reset_index(drop=True)
    outs['TradeDir']   = ins['TradeDir'].values[:len(outs)]
    outs['EntryPrice'] = ins['EntryPrice'].values[:len(outs)]
    outs['Comment']    = outs['Comment'].astype(str)
    outs['ExitReason'] = outs['Comment'].apply(lambda x: 'TP' if 'tp' in x.lower() else ('BE' if 'be' in x.lower() else ('SL' if 'sl' in x.lower() else 'Other')))
    outs['DayOfWeek']  = outs['DateTime'].dt.day_name()
    outs['Month']      = outs['DateTime'].dt.month
    outs['Year']       = outs['DateTime'].dt.year
    outs['Win']        = outs['Profit'] > 0
    return outs

def stats(df):
    if len(df)==0: return {'n':0,'wr':0,'pf':0,'net':0,'avgW':0,'avgL':0}
    wins=df['Win'].sum(); total=len(df)
    gw=df[df['Profit']>0]['Profit'].sum()
    gl=abs(df[df['Profit']<0]['Profit'].sum())
    pf=gw/gl if gl>0 else 999
    avgW=df[df['Profit']>0]['Profit'].mean() if wins>0 else 0
    avgL=df[df['Profit']<0]['Profit'].mean() if (total-wins)>0 else 0
    return {'n':total,'wr':wins/total*100,'pf':pf,'net':df['Profit'].sum(),'avgW':avgW,'avgL':avgL}

def max_dd_and_streak(df):
    profits = df.sort_values('DateTime')['Profit'].values
    balances = df.sort_values('DateTime')['Balance'].values
    peak=0; maxdd=0; running=0
    for p in profits:
        running+=p
        if running>peak: peak=running
        dd=peak-running
        if dd>maxdd: maxdd=dd
    streaks=[]; curr=0
    for p in profits:
        if p<0: curr+=1
        else:
            if curr>0: streaks.append(curr)
            curr=0
    if curr>0: streaks.append(curr)
    return maxdd, max(streaks) if streaks else 0

files = {'S1_BK':'set1_bk','S1_FR':'set1_fr','S2_BK':'set2_bk','S2_FR':'set2_fr'}
data = {k: extract_trades(f'/mnt/user-data/uploads/{v}.xlsx') for k,v in files.items()}

months = ['Jan','Feb','Mar','Apr','May','Jun','Jul','Aug','Sep','Oct','Nov','Dec']
days   = ['Monday','Tuesday','Wednesday','Thursday','Friday']

print("="*60)
print("GLOBAL STATS")
for k,df in data.items():
    s=stats(df); mdd,mls=max_dd_and_streak(df)
    print(f"{k}: n={s['n']} WR={s['wr']:.1f}% PF={s['pf']:.3f} Net=${s['net']:.0f} DD=${mdd:.0f}({mdd/5000*100:.2f}%) MaxLS={mls} AvgW=${s['avgW']:.1f} AvgL=${s['avgL']:.1f}")

print("\n" + "="*60)
print("MONTHLY EQUITY")
for k in ['S1_BK','S1_FR','S2_BK','S2_FR']:
    df=data[k]
    print(f"\n{k}:")
    for y in sorted(df['Year'].unique()):
        for m in range(1,13):
            sub=df[(df['Year']==y)&(df['Month']==m)]
            if len(sub)==0: continue
            s=stats(sub)
            sign='+' if s['net']>=0 else ''
            print(f"  {y}-{months[m-1]}: {sign}${s['net']:.0f}  WR={s['wr']:.0f}%  n={s['n']}")

print("\n" + "="*60)
print("DAY OF WEEK")
for k in ['S1_BK','S1_FR','S2_BK','S2_FR']:
    df=data[k]; print(f"\n{k}:")
    for day in days:
        s=stats(df[df['DayOfWeek']==day])
        if s['n']==0: continue
        print(f"  {day}: n={s['n']} WR={s['wr']:.1f}% PF={s['pf']:.3f} Net=${s['net']:.0f}")

print("\n" + "="*60)
print("DIRECTION BUY/SELL")
for k in ['S1_BK','S1_FR','S2_BK','S2_FR']:
    df=data[k]; print(f"\n{k}:")
    for d in ['buy','sell']:
        s=stats(df[df['TradeDir']==d])
        print(f"  {d.upper()}: n={s['n']} WR={s['wr']:.1f}% PF={s['pf']:.3f} Net=${s['net']:.0f}")

print("\n" + "="*60)
print("EXIT REASONS")
for k,df in data.items():
    print(f"{k}: {df['ExitReason'].value_counts().to_dict()}")

print("\n" + "="*60)
print("WINNING MONTHS")
for k in ['S1_BK','S1_FR','S2_BK','S2_FR']:
    df=data[k]
    monthly=df.groupby(['Year','Month'])['Profit'].sum()
    pos=(monthly>0).sum(); neg=(monthly<=0).sum()
    print(f"{k}: {pos} positivos / {neg} negativos de {len(monthly)} total")

print("\n" + "="*60)
print("DAY x DIRECTION")
for k in ['S1_BK','S1_FR','S2_BK','S2_FR']:
    df=data[k]; print(f"\n{k}:")
    for day in days:
        for d in ['buy','sell']:
            s=stats(df[(df['DayOfWeek']==day)&(df['TradeDir']==d)])
            if s['n']==0: continue
            print(f"  {day[:3]} {d.upper()}: n={s['n']} WR={s['wr']:.1f}% PF={s['pf']:.3f} Net=${s['net']:.0f}")

print("\n" + "="*60)
print("QUARTERLY")
for k in ['S1_BK','S1_FR','S2_BK','S2_FR']:
    df=data[k]; print(f"\n{k}:")
    df2=df.copy(); df2['Q']=((df2['Month']-1)//3)+1
    for q in [1,2,3,4]:
        s=stats(df2[df2['Q']==q])
        if s['n']==0: continue
        print(f"  Q{q}: n={s['n']} WR={s['wr']:.1f}% PF={s['pf']:.3f} Net=${s['net']:.0f}")
EOF
